title: NRC, Agreement States, and License Reciprocity
narrationScript: Every radiography source you will ever touch is possessed under a specific license, and that license comes from exactly one regulator. This lesson maps who that regulator is and how your license travels. Congress put radioactive material under federal control with the Atomic Energy Act of 1954, and the Nuclear Regulatory Commission has been its independent regulator since 1975. Under Section 274b of the Act, the NRC can transfer its materials authority to a state whose program is adequate to protect public health and safety and compatible with the NRC's own. About forty states have done it — the Agreement States. Your gamma sources answer to one of those two regulators. X-ray machines are different: states register machines; the NRC never licenses them. When work crosses a jurisdictional line, reciprocity is how the license follows. File NRC Form 241 at least three days ahead for NRC jurisdiction, or the host state's equivalent form, pay the fee, and respect the 180-day annual cap. Cross the line without filing and you are operating unlicensed. Plan jurisdiction the way you plan dose.

sections.heading: One License, One Regulator — Why Jurisdiction Matters
sections.body:
Every curie of iridium-192 your company owns is possessed under a **specific license**, and that license is issued by exactly one regulator. Which regulator depends on where your program is based and where the work happens — and getting that question wrong turns a routine pipe-weld job into unlicensed possession of radioactive material. Jurisdiction is an operational skill, checked during job planning right alongside source activity and boundary dose rates.

The framework begins with the **Atomic Energy Act of 1954 (AEA)**, the federal statute that placed radioactive materials under national control. Its first steward, the Atomic Energy Commission, both promoted and regulated atomic energy — a built-in conflict that the **Energy Reorganization Act of 1974** resolved by splitting the agency. The **U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC)** began work in 1975 as the independent regulator of civilian radioactive-materials use. The NRC writes its requirements in **Title 10 of the Code of Federal Regulations (10 CFR)**, issues and amends licenses, inspects licensees, and enforces with notices of violation, civil penalties, and orders — up to and including shutting a radiography program down.

sections.heading: AEA Materials — and the X-Ray Machine Exception
sections.body:
Federal authority follows the **material**, not the radiation. The AEA covers three classes:

- **Byproduct material** — material made radioactive in a reactor. Every common radiography isotope — **Ir-192, Co-60, Se-75** — is produced by neutron activation, so every gamma source you will ever load is byproduct material. (In 2005, Congress broadened the definition to add discrete radium-226 sources and accelerator-produced radioactive material.)
- **Source material** — uranium and thorium. The **depleted-uranium shield** inside many exposure devices is source material, which is why it appears on the license right beside the sources.
- **Special nuclear material** — plutonium and enriched uranium; licensed under the AEA but not part of radiography.

**X-ray machines are the exception.** A crawler or a directional tube head produces radiation electrically and contains no AEA material, so the NRC has no authority over it. Radiation-producing **machines** are regulated by the **states** — every state operates a radiation control program that registers X-ray equipment and sets machine rules, most modeled on the Suggested State Regulations published by the Conference of Radiation Control Program Directors. One truck can therefore carry two sets of paperwork: a materials license for the projector, and a state machine registration for the crawler.

sections.caption: The federal rulebook for a radiography program — and what each part does on the job.
headers: What it means on the job
rows: Notices, instructions, and reports to workers
You must be informed you are a radiation worker, have access to posted documents, and may speak with inspectors and request an inspection
Occupational and public dose limits, ALARA, surveys, posting, dosimetry, and records
The licensing chassis that every materials license sits on
Equipment performance, radiographer certification, supervision, surveys, and field operating rules
Physical protection of Category 1 and 2 quantities
Typical radiography sources are Category 2 — access control, trustworthiness reviews, and source tracking
NRC package standards plus DOT carriage rules govern every source shipment (detailed later in this module)
§150.20 — how an Agreement State license operates inside NRC jurisdiction

sections.heading: Agreement States: Federal Authority, Formally Transferred
sections.body:
In 1959, Congress added **Section 274** to the Atomic Energy Act. Under **§274b**, the NRC may sign a formal agreement with a state and relinquish its regulatory authority over byproduct material, source material, and special nuclear material in quantities below a critical mass, within that state's borders. The state then licenses, inspects, and enforces in place of the NRC.

The transfer is earned, not assumed. The governor requests it, and the state must show enabling legislation, a funded program with qualified staff, and regulations **adequate** to protect public health and safety and **compatible** with the NRC's program. Compatible does not mean word-for-word identical — rule numbering differs, and a state may be stricter in places — but the core protections must match. The NRC audits every Agreement State on a continuing cycle through **IMPEP**, the Integrated Materials Performance Evaluation Program, and can place a weak program under heightened oversight or, in the extreme, suspend or terminate the agreement.

About **40 of the 50 states** hold agreements today, and the roster grows over time — verify any state's current status with the NRC or the state program before relying on it. The practical effect for a radiography company: your specific license comes either from the NRC or from your home Agreement State — never both — and your daily rulebook is whichever regulator issued it.

sections.caption: Authority follows the material and the location: AEA materials answer to the NRC or an Agreement State, while X-ray machines are registered with state radiation control programs everywhere.

sections.title: Federal islands inside Agreement States
sections.body: An Agreement State's authority stops at the boundary of **exclusive federal jurisdiction** — many military installations and certain other federal sites. Inside those areas, AEA materials answer to the **NRC**, even though the site sits within the state that issued your license. A shot on such a base in your own state is an NRC-jurisdiction job that requires a reciprocity filing first. Jurisdiction status varies installation by installation and cannot be read off the gate sign — confirm it with the facility and the regulator during job planning.

sections.heading: Reciprocity: How a License Travels
sections.body:
A specific license is territorial — it authorizes work only where its issuer has authority. **Reciprocity** is the host jurisdiction's standing offer of a **general license** to a visiting company that already holds a specific license somewhere else. It is activated by filing, never by assumption.

**Into NRC jurisdiction** — a non-Agreement State, offshore waters, or an area of exclusive federal jurisdiction — the rule is **10 CFR 150.20**. Provided your Agreement State license authorizes work at temporary job sites, you file **NRC Form 241**, *Report of Proposed Activities in Non-Agreement States, Areas of Exclusive Federal Jurisdiction, or Offshore Waters*, with a copy of your license and the required fee, **at least 3 days before work begins**, and you file revisions when locations or dates change. While there, you comply with the applicable NRC regulations — and the clock matters: the general license covers **no more than 180 days per calendar year** of work in NRC jurisdiction. Beyond that, a specific NRC license is required.

**Into another Agreement State**, the pattern mirrors: file that state's reciprocity request, pay its fee, and give its required advance notice — commonly around 3 days, though forms, fees, notice periods, and annual limits vary state to state, so verify with the host program before dispatch. Note carefully what reciprocity moves: the **company license**. Your personal radiographer certification — an IRRSP-type card issued through an independent certifying entity under Part 34 and its state equivalents — is recognized across jurisdictions, but it never substitutes for the company's filing.

sections.caption: The reciprocity decision path: identify the host jurisdiction, file before the truck rolls, and watch the 180-day calendar-year ceiling.

sections.title: No filing, no shot
sections.body: Radiography across a jurisdictional line without a reciprocity filing is possession and use of licensed material **without a license** — one of the fastest routes to escalated enforcement: notices of violation, civil penalties, and orders that stop work. Inspectors do meet reciprocity crews in the field; the Form 241 you filed tells them where to find you, so keep its locations and dates current. Build the jurisdiction check into every out-of-area bid: Which state? Agreement or not? Federal enclave? How many reciprocity days has the company used this calendar year? When any answer is unclear, call the NRC regional office or the host state program **before** dispatch.

figures.title: Who regulates industrial radiography: NRC, Agreement States, and the machine exception
Reciprocity decision path for out-of-jurisdiction jobs

interactive.title: Three Jobs, Three Jurisdictions
interactive.intro: Your company holds a specific radiography license from its home Agreement State, authorizing work at temporary job sites. You help the RSO plan out-of-area work. Make the calls that keep every shot licensed.

steps.prompt: Dispatch books a five-day pipeline job starting next week in a non-Agreement State. What must happen before the crew can work there?
options: Nothing — a state-issued radiography license is valid anywhere in the United States
File NRC Form 241 with a copy of the license and the required fee at least 3 days before work begins
Apply for a new specific NRC license and wait for it to be issued
Send the customer's RSO a copy of your license and proof of radiographer certification
steps.feedback: In NRC jurisdiction, 10 CFR 150.20 grants a visiting Agreement State licensee a general license — but only once NRC Form 241, a copy of the license, and the fee are filed at least 3 days ahead. No new specific license is needed for short-term work, and no customer paperwork substitutes for the filing.

steps.prompt: The next request is a weld job at a military depot that is an area of exclusive federal jurisdiction — located inside your own home state. Who gets the filing?
options: No one — your home-state license already covers everything inside the state line
The base commander — written site permission replaces licensing on federal property
The NRC — state authority does not extend into exclusive federal jurisdiction, so Form 241 is filed even though you never leave your state
The Department of Defense, which licenses all radiography on military installations
steps.feedback: Exclusive federal jurisdiction is an island the Agreement State's authority does not reach. AEA materials there answer to the NRC, so the job needs a reciprocity filing exactly as if it were in a non-Agreement State. Site access permission is also needed, but it never substitutes for the license.

steps.prompt: By October, the company has logged 176 days of reciprocity work in NRC jurisdiction this calendar year. A customer offers a 12-day turnaround job in a non-Agreement State in November. What is the right call?
options: Reciprocity cannot cover this job — past 180 days in the calendar year the general license ends, so the company needs a specific NRC license or must decline
File a fresh Form 241 — each filing grants its own 180 days
Take the job — only days a source is actually exposed count against the limit
Run the first four days under reciprocity and the rest under the customer's license
steps.feedback: The 180-day ceiling in 10 CFR 150.20 is an aggregate cap per calendar year across all work in NRC jurisdiction. It does not reset with each filing, it is not measured in exposure time, and you cannot borrow another company's license. Companies that routinely brush the cap pursue their own specific NRC license.

steps.prompt: The same trip adds X-ray crawler work in a neighboring Agreement State. The state reciprocity request for your gamma sources is already filed. What about the crawler?
options: Covered — a materials reciprocity filing extends to all radiation work on the job
Nothing is required — X-ray machines are exempt from regulation
Register the machine work with that state's radiation control program under its X-ray rules — AEA reciprocity never covers machines
File NRC Form 241 for the crawler as well
steps.feedback: Reciprocity is an AEA-materials mechanism. X-ray machines contain no licensed material, so each state regulates them through its own registration program, and out-of-state machine work has its own notification requirements that vary by state. The NRC has no role in machine registration.

checkQuestions.question: A radiography truck carries an Ir-192 projector and a 250 kV X-ray crawler. Which statement about regulatory authority is correct?
options: Both are licensed by the NRC or an Agreement State, because both produce ionizing radiation
The X-ray machine is federally licensed, while the Ir-192 source is registered with the state
The Ir-192 source is licensed by the NRC or an Agreement State as byproduct material; the X-ray machine is registered with the state radiation control program
Neither needs licensing or registration as long as dose limits are never exceeded
checkQuestions.explanation: Atomic Energy Act authority follows the material: reactor-produced Ir-192 is byproduct material and requires a specific license from the NRC or an Agreement State. A machine produces radiation electrically and contains no AEA material, so it falls to the state's machine-registration program — in every state.

checkQuestions.question: Before the NRC relinquishes materials authority to a state under AEA Section 274b, the state's radiation control program must be found:
options: Adequate to protect public health and safety, and compatible with the NRC's program
Identical, word for word, to the NRC's regulations in 10 CFR
Approved by a majority vote of the licensees operating in that state
Capable of regulating nuclear power reactors as well as materials
checkQuestions.explanation: The governor requests the agreement, and the state must demonstrate a program that is adequate and compatible — the statutory standard. Compatibility allows differences in numbering and detail (a state may even be stricter), and reactor regulation is never transferred; it stays federal.

checkQuestions.question: Under 10 CFR 150.20, an Agreement State licensee starting its first reciprocity job of the year in a non-Agreement State must:
options: Obtain a new specific NRC license before any work, regardless of job duration
File NRC Form 241, a copy of its Agreement State license, and the required fee at least 3 days before beginning work
Notify the customer's radiation safety officer in writing at least 30 days ahead
Do nothing — an Agreement State license applies automatically nationwide
checkQuestions.explanation: Section 150.20 grants the visitor an NRC general license, activated by filing Form 241 with a license copy and fee at least 3 days before work begins — and by filing revisions when sites or dates change. A specific license is territorial; it never applies automatically outside its issuer's jurisdiction.

checkQuestions.question: A company has already worked 178 days under reciprocity in NRC jurisdiction this calendar year. For a new 10-day job in a non-Agreement State in December, the 180-day limit means:
options: Nothing — the count restarts with every new job
The job is allowed if a new Form 241 is filed, which grants a fresh 180 days
The job is allowed because only days with actual source exposures count
Reciprocity cannot cover the work — beyond 180 days in the calendar year, a specific NRC license is required
checkQuestions.explanation: The 180-day allowance is an aggregate ceiling per calendar year for all activity in NRC jurisdiction under the general license. It does not reset per job or per filing, so a company at 178 days cannot complete a 10-day job under reciprocity.

checkQuestions.question: A radiography job sits on a military installation that is an area of exclusive federal jurisdiction, inside the same Agreement State that issued the company's license. Regulatory authority over the job belongs to:
options: The Agreement State, because the installation is within its borders
The NRC — Agreement State authority does not extend into exclusive federal jurisdiction, so a Form 241 reciprocity filing is required first
The installation's commanding officer, in place of any civilian regulator
Both regulators jointly, with two specific licenses required
checkQuestions.explanation: Exclusive federal jurisdiction is carved out of the state's authority, so AEA materials there fall back to the NRC. Even in its home state, the licensee files Form 241 under 10 CFR 150.20 before working on such a site — base permission controls access, not licensing.

references: Atomic Energy Act of 1954, Section 274 (state agreements program)
10 CFR Part 150, §150.20 — recognition of Agreement State licenses (NRC Form 241 reciprocity)
10 CFR Part 34 — licenses for industrial radiography and radiation safety requirements for industrial radiographic operations
CP-105-2024 Radiation Safety Outline §12.0–12.2 (state and federal regulations; NRC and Agreement States; license reciprocity)
